goal_07.config.title: Access to electricity: who remains without power?
goal_07.config.abstract: Global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, yet progress remains deeply uneven. While most regions have achieved near-universal coverage, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to lag significantly behind, with rural communities facing the greatest barriers. Initiatives like Mission 300 aim to extend electricity to  300 million more people in Africa by 2030.
goal_07.config.citation: Tony Fujs, Haruna Kashiwase, and Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. “Access to electricity: who remains without power?” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by D. Mahler, A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
goal_07.config.homeFactoidText: people in Sub-Saharan Africa live without electricity today
goal_07.config.homeTagline: Global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, yet millions in Sub-Saharan Africa's rural areas remain left behind.
goal_07.content.#1.text: Reliable electricity access is important for improving quality of life. It contributes to increasing comfort, but also supports work productivity, connectivity, education, and the provision of health services. Lack of access to electricity severely limits opportunities to learn, work, and thrive.
goal_07.content.#2.title: People without access to electricity
goal_07.content.#2.label: In 2023, globally
goal_07.content.#3.text: Electricity  enables students to study after dark, ensures storage and provision of vaccines and medical supplies, powers irrigation, enables modern industrial production and job creation [reference: job_creation] – all essential drivers of economic and social development.  Yet, 666 million people still live without electricity, and many more have limited or unreliable supply.
goal_07.content.#4.text: Global gains in access, but uneven progress
goal_07.content.#5.text: Access to electricity has increased almost continuously over the past two decades. Many regions have achieved near-universal access, but still large gaps remain elsewhere.
goal_07.content.#6.title: Electricity access is advancing globally and across all regions
goal_07.content.#6.subtitle: Share of people with access to electricity, by region (%)
goal_07.content.#6.y_axis_title: Share of people with access to electricity
goal_07.content.#6.source: SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset, World Bank (WB). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EG.ELC.ACCS.ZS).
goal_07.content.#6.visdescription: This chart describes time series trendlines of electricity access by regions
goal_07.content.#6.keyFactLabel: of the world’s population had access to electricity in 2023.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#0.text: Global access to electricity has expanded steadily over the past two decades. By 2023, more than 90 percent of the world’s population had access to electricity—up from 78 percent in 2000.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#1.text: Most regions already had very high levels of access in 2000, and had achieved near-universal access to electricity by 2023. Two regions, South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa lagged noticeably behind in 2000.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#2.text: But South Asia made dramatic progress. In 2000, South Asia lagged far behind the global average with less than 60 percent of the population having access to electricity. But by 2021, the region reached near universal access.
goal_07.content.#6.scenes.#3.text: Sub-Saharan Africa started from an even lower base in 2000 (about 26 percent). In the next two decades, access doubled. Still, only about half of the region’s population had electricity by 2023.
goal_07.content.#7.text: While progress has been remarkable, this indicator captures only the most basic level of electricity access. It measures the share of people whose primary source of lighting at home is electricity, whether from the grid, solar home systems, mini-grids, or other stand-alone renewable sources. Having a connection, however, does not guarantee that electricity is reliable, affordable, or sufficient to power appliances and support productive uses[footnote: If you would like to see the different levels of electricity access defined by Multi-Tier Framework (MTF), please see the 2023 SDG Atlas: https://datatopics.worldbank.org/sdgatlas/goal-7-affordable-and-clean-energy/?lang=en] Still, reaching universal access at this basic level is a critical milestone. It is the first step toward ensuring that everyone can benefit from modern energy services.
goal_07.content.#8.text: Has this remarkable progress translated into a decline in the total number of people without electricity access?
goal_07.content.#9.title: Fewer people without electricity, even as global population grows
goal_07.content.#9.source: Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP). SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.
goal_07.content.#9.note: Regional estimates of the population without access to electricity are calculated by World Bank staff.
goal_07.content.#9.visdescription: This chart decomposes global access to electricity by regions, zoom in on Sub-Saharan Africa, and decomposes Sub-Saharan Access by country.
goal_07.content.#9.keyFactLabel: of the 666 million people without access to electricity live in Sub-Saharan Africa.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#0.text: While the global population has increased by almost 2 billion people since 2000…
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#1.text: … the number of people without electricity halved, falling from 1.3 billion in 2000 to 666 million in 2023.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#2.text: All regions except Sub-Saharan Africa contributed to this rapid decline, in particular South Asia. In 2000, both South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa accounted for roughly the same share of people without electricity. By 2023 South Asia achieved near-universal access. But at the same time, the number of people without electricity in Sub-Saharan Africa increased. Despite significant progress in access, it was not enough to keep up with population growth.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#3.text: In 2023, Sub-Saharan Africa was home to 581 million people without electricity. This translates to nearly 9 out of 10 of all people living without electricity worldwide.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#4.text: Just eight countries in Sub-Saharan Africa account for half of all people who still lack access to electricity.
goal_07.content.#9.scenes.#5.text: And over one-third of the global population without electricity resides in just three countries: Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Ethiopia, the region’s most populous nations.
goal_07.content.#10.text: Where electricity access still lags: rural Sub-Saharan Africa
goal_07.content.#11.text: Urban areas reached near‑universal electricity access long before rural ones. In 2000, already 94 percent of urban residents had electricity, compared with just 66 percent of people in rural areas. By 2023, rural access had climbed to around 83 percent, significantly narrowing the gap.
goal_07.content.#12.text: In most regions, urban and rural access rates have gradually moved closer together. But Sub-Saharan Africa stands out as a persistent outlier. In 2023, only one in three rural residents in Sub-Saharan Africa had access to electricity, compared with more than four in five people living in urban areas.
goal_07.content.#13.title: Rural areas have less access to electricity
goal_07.content.#13.subtitle: Share of people with access to electricity (%)
goal_07.content.#13.source: SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset, World Bank (WB). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EG.ELC.ACCS.ZS, EG.ELC.ACCS.RU.ZS, EG.ELC.ACCS.UR.ZS)
goal_07.content.#13.visdescription: This chart shows the difference of access between urban and rural areas in different regions.
goal_07.content.#14.text: The persistence of this gap in Sub‑Saharan Africa reflects the higher costs per connection and constraints in affordability among sparsely populated, low‑income rural areas. Homes are widely dispersed, driving up infrastructure costs, while lower electricity use in rural areas limits revenues for providers. The underlying economics thus represents a challenge to service extension into remote rural areas.[reference: wb_electrifying_africa] Planning and regulatory shortcomings have also played a role in delaying rural electrification and the wider deployment of off‑grid options. [reference: wb_ssa_access]
goal_07.content.#15.text: Progress in Nigeria, the DRC, and Ethiopia and remaining disparities
goal_07.content.#16.text: Among the 30 countries with the largest populations lacking electricity, 24 are in Sub-Saharan Africa. Three countries —[emphasis: Nigeria], [emphasis: the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)], and [emphasis: Ethiopia]—account for around a third of the world’s population without electricity access.
goal_07.content.#17.text: While all three countries host large populations without access to electricity, their national contexts differ considerably. The share of the population without[emphasis:  ]access ranges from around 40 percent in Nigeria and 45 percent in Ethiopia to 80 percent  in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
goal_07.content.#18.text: Their electrification trajectories also diverge. Between 2015 and 2023, progress in Nigeria and the DRC lagged behind that of comparable countries, reflecting slow and uneven gains. Ethiopia, by contrast, made substantial strides, expanding electricity access at nearly twice the pace of its peers (see this explainer for a detailed description of the methodology on progress used in the Atlas).
goal_07.content.#19.title: Ethiopia outpaced Nigeria and the DRC in progressing towards universal electrification
goal_07.content.#19.subtitle: Indicator: share of people with access to electricity (% of population)
goal_07.content.#19.source: World Bank staff calculations, based on XXXX 2026 (To be added when working paper available)
goal_07.content.#19.visdescription: This chart shows how the speed of progress in achieving electricity access varies between countries. It focuses on Ethiopia, DRC, and Nigeria.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#0.text: This shows access to electricity levels in 2015. Both Ethiopia and the Democratic Republic of Congo were far behind Nigeria.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#1.text: In 2023, while DRC’s share of the population with access to electricity had only increased slightly, Ethiopia had almost caught-up with Nigeria.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#2.text: Ethiopia is one of the countries that progressed the fastest over the 2015-2023 period. Nigeria and DRC, on the other hand, lagged behind comparable countries.
goal_07.content.#19.scenes.#3.text: Now explore all countries by hovering over the arrow heads, or by searching for a country.
goal_07.content.#20.text: In all three countries, electricity access for rural residents lags behind urban residents. In the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), electricity access among rural populations remained extremely low—at around 1 percent between 2015 and 2023. In Nigeria, the levels were not as low as the DRC, however, little has changed in both rural and urban areas. By contrast, Ethiopia made rapid progress in expanding electricity access in rural areas. From nearly no access, almost half of the rural population today have access to electricity. Access for urban residents in Ethiopia was fairly high in 2000 and by 2023, had reached near universal access. Although gaps still remain, there was notable reduction in the urban-rural access gap.
goal_07.content.#21.title: Rapid rural progress in Ethiopia reduces the urban–rural gap while progress remains limited in DRC and Nigeria
goal_07.content.#21.subtitle: Share of people with access to electricity, by place of residence (%)
goal_07.content.#21.source: SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset, World Bank (WB). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (EG.ELC.ACCS.RU.ZS, EG.ELC.ACCS.UR.ZS)
goal_07.content.#21.visdescription: This chart shows the trajectory of electricity access between urban and rural areas in Ethiopia, Nigeria, and DRC.
goal_07.content.#21.keyFactLabel: of rural dwellers in Ethiopia had access to electricity in 2023, up from just 2 percent in 2000.
goal_07.content.#22.text: Electricity access and the infrastructure that supports it vary widely between densely connected urban areas and more isolated rural regions. In cities, people are usually connected to a national electricity grid, while in remote rural areas many households still rely on small solar systems or have no connection at all. Even where households have access to electricity, the quality of supply can vary widely: some places have stable power all day, while others experience frequent blackouts, low voltage, or power that only works for a few hours at a time.
goal_07.content.#23.text: Let’s now examine electricity access at a more granular level, drawing on household surveys and geospatial data to better understand the dynamics of electricity access in Ethiopia and Nigeria, two countries with some of the largest populations without electricity.
goal_07.content.#24.title: Uneven and unreliable access in Nigeria
goal_07.content.#24.source: World Bank, Development Data Group (DECDG). (2026). Space2Stats (S2S): Subnational Geospatial Indicators (Version 1.0). Washington, DC: World Bank. Available at https://worldbank.github.io/DECAT_Space2Stats/readme.html (accessed: 2026‑02‑20).
goal_07.content.#24.visdescription: This visualization shows a map of Nightlights in Nigeria as a proxy for electricity access.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: Electricity access in Nigeria is highly uneven]. Southern zones enjoy relatively high coverage, while large parts of the north lag far behind. These disparities reflect the concentration of grid infrastructure and population in the south, along with differences in investment, urbanization, and political stability, with conflicts that disrupted large parts of northern Nigeria in recent years.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#1.text: [emphasis: Nighttime lights provide a more granular view of electricity access]. They are a proxy for electricity access and usage. When overlaid on the map, they show where power is actually being consumed.[footnote: Nighttime lights cannot capture electricity use by solar panels, as they do not produce electricity at night. They are therefore a closer proxy of grid connection and use.]
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#2.text: The[emphasis: ]brightest clusters correspond to major cities such as Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt, where grid access is widespread and power consumption is relatively high. In contrast, vast rural and peri-urban areas appear weak or dark—signaling either a lack of access or unreliable supply.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#3.text: Let’s focus on[emphasis:  urban areas ]of Nigeria…
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#4.text: … and zoom in on the city of Kano. As in most cities, a large majority of households in Kano are connected to the grid. Neighbourhoods where bright lights can be detected are likely well served with access to reliable electricity.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#5.text: Filtering out the brightest data points highlights that, even when connected to the grid, [emphasis: large urban and suburban zones in Kano may remain underserved, as nighttime lights appear ][emphasis: weak][emphasis: or ][emphasis: dark][emphasis: .]
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#6.text: These areas are densely populated, suburban settlements but still lack highly visible illuminations.
goal_07.content.#24.scenes.#7.text: For example, this 36 square kilometer cell is estimated to house more than 100,000 people, but remains largely in the dark. This contrast illustrates that, even though  most urban areas are connected to the grid, quality of access is not yet universal and [emphasis: grid reliability is far from consistent].
goal_07.content.#25.text: Grid unreliability is a defining feature of electricity access in Nigeria. According to the 2023/24 Nigeria General Household Survey – Panel (Wave 5)[reference: nigeria_survey], blackouts are frequent and prolonged across much of the country. At the national level, households reported about seven outages per week, with a typical duration of 12 hours each. To cope, many Nigerians alternate between grid power and generators. Petrol and diesel generators are ubiquitous in both urban and rural settings. While they provide an essential backup, they are also financially burdensome, and a source of toxic emissions harmful to people and ecosystems. [reference: nigeria_blog]
goal_07.content.#26.text: Similar to Nigeria, electricity access in Ethiopia remains uneven, and urban centers like Addis Ababa and regional capitals benefit from the highest access level through grid connection. In urban centers 92 percent of the population relied on grid lines as their primary electricity source in 2022.[reference: ethiopia_survey] But even in urban areas, access is not yet universal, with five percent of Ethiopians in urban areas (1.7 million people) not having access to electricity.
goal_07.content.#27.title: Electricity access progresses in rural Ethiopia
goal_07.content.#27.subtitle: Solar electricity is a key factor of progress in rural areas
goal_07.content.#27.visdescription: This visualization shows a map of Nightlights in Ethiopia as a proxy for electricity access. It focused on access progress in rural areas.
goal_07.content.#27.newly_lit_2023: Dark areas in 2012 that had some night lights in 2023
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#0.text: Let’s focus on Ethiopia’s [emphasis: rural areas only][footnote: urban areas are left blank to bring focus to nighttime lights view in rural areas.][emphasis: ]and take a look at the nighttime lights view of 2012. Areas where at least some light (weak or strong) was detected are shown in light blue, whereas areas where no nighttime light was detected are shown in dark blue. While western parts of rural Ethiopia had some nighttime lights coverage in 2012, eastern and southern rural Ethiopia remained largely in the dark.
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#1.text: The power grid lines reached only a few rural areas in 2012. So how did the illuminated rural areas have access to electricity? About 75 percent of the [emphasis: rural population] in Ethiopia with access consumed electricity not through power grid lines, but through[emphasis: solar power.][reference: ethiopia_survey]
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#2.text: Let’s focus on the [emphasis: south-east part of the country] which is more sparsely populated and remained mostly in the dark in 2012[emphasis: .]
goal_07.content.#27.scenes.#3.text: By 2023, a lot more nighttime lights could be detected in the south-eastern region of Ethiopia. Much of this expansion in access to electricity happened due to the [emphasis: rapid uptake of solar electricity][reference: ethiopia_survey]. This expansion of solar power helped reduce the persistent gap in access to electricity in rural areas.
goal_07.content.#28.text: While access reliability may still be an issue  in Ethiopia, significant improvements have been achieved over the past years, with some regions experiencing a  50 percent decrease in power outages over the period 2019 – 2022. [reference: ethiopia_survey]
goal_07.content.#30.text: Despite significant global progress in electricity access, major gaps remain. Sub-Saharan Africa now accounts for the vast majority of people still without electricity, with 581 million people still lacking electricity access in 2023. Even within the region, a small number of countries account for most of this deficit. Third, spatial inequalities endure, with rural areas persistently lagging behind urban centers.
goal_07.content.#31.text: Now, the challenge of electricity access is predominantly concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa, where resources and efforts must focus to reach universal access. Within the region, the nature of access gaps varies widely across and within countries, reflecting differences in geography, infrastructure, utility performance, and regulatory environments. Closing the divide will therefore require context-specific approaches that align local conditions with appropriate technical and financing solutions.
goal_07.content.#32.text: The World Bank, working with regional institutions and governments, is helping close these gaps. In 2024, the World Bank and the African Development Bank launched Mission 300 [reference: mission300], a joint initiative to accelerate universal access in Sub-Saharan Africa by providing electricity to 300 million people by 2030—effectively halving the number currently without power. Mission 300 will expand grid connections where they are economically viable, strengthen utilities to improve service quality and financial sustainability, and scale decentralized renewables—such as mini-grids and solar home systems—to reach communities beyond the grid.
goal_07.references.job_creation: Lehr, Ulrike. 2023. Occupational Choice and Energy Access – Electricity For More And Better Jobs. Jobs Working Paper; Issue No.77. © World Bank. http://hdl.handle.net/10986/40629 License: CC BY 3.0 IGO

goal_07.references.esmap_data: Original data sources are available in the ESMAP dataset file. For methodological details, see the SDG Indicators Metadata Repository: https://unstats.un.org/sdgs/metadata/files/Metadata-07-01-01.pdf
goal_07.references.nigeria_survey: National Bureau of Statistics, Nigeria. 2023/24 Nigeria General Household Survey – Panel (Wave 5).
goal_07.references.nigeria_blog: World Bank. (2023, December 15). Nigeria to expand access to clean energy for 17.5 million people. World Bank.
goal_07.references.ethiopia_survey: Ethiopian Statistical Service (ESS) and World Bank. 2023. Ethiopia Socioeconomic Panel Survey (ESPS) Report - Wave 5, 2021/22.
